camu.analysis.digest: presents an internal menu for choices of analysis and segmentation and loads the corresponding camu.process.<choice>.maxpat in a bpatcher to show the analysis parameters.
camu.analysis.use_when: Audio analysis dispatcher — presents an internal menu of available analysis types (segmentation algorithms etc.) and loads the corresponding `camu.process.<choice>.maxpat` as a bpatcher to expose its parameters. Constructor args: `<corpus> <audio> <descr>` (corpus name, audio buffer name, descriptor track name). The internal menu can be slaved to an outside menu via the right outlet. Send `<symbol>` or `<index>` to switch analysis subpatch programmatically. Use as the entry point for audio→corpus analysis pipelines in catart-mubu.

camu.cont.digest: Each selection (by move or click) restarts the loop. camu.cont needs to be connected to the rightmost outlet of mubu.concat~. Stop the loop by switching to fence mode.
camu.cont.use_when: Continuous-trigger handler — keeps mubu.concat~ looping grains when in 'cont' trigger mode. Connect to the **rightmost** outlet of mubu.concat~. The selected mubu.concat~ must have `@outputstate 1` to expose the state stream camu.cont needs. Switch to fence mode in camu.triggermode to stop the loop. Constructor args: `<corpus> <descr>`. Use as the loop-driver inside a catart-mubu voice when you want continuous (rather than per-event) grain triggering.

camu.control.digest: for devices able to display the corpus, the arguments are: mubu name and descriptor track
camu.control.use_when: Multi-touch device input adapter — formats touch input as ROLI-multi-touch lists (touch index, phase 0/1/2, X 0–1, Y 0–1) for driving the corpus interface. Constructor args: `<mubu-name> <descr-track>`. Use to drive camu.imubu.control from a multi-touch device (Roli Lightpad, etc.). Note: not yet poly~-based, so true simultaneous multi-touch is limited.

camu.filenamenotenumber.digest: important: the const module at the end of descr:chop:const creates a descriptor column and gives it a name by @const.name FilenameNoteNumber
camu.filenamenotenumber.use_when: Extracts pitch from soundfile filenames and exposes it as a descriptor column for transposition matching. Required as part of the descr:chop:const pipeline — the const module at the end gives the descriptor column its name (`@const.name FilenameNoteNumber`). Constructor args: `<corpus> <descr-track> <output-column-name|index>`. Filenames with no detectable pitch get value 0; downstream transposition modules flag those for bypass. Use when corpus filenames encode pitch (e.g. `piano_C4.wav`) and you want pitch-aware retrieval.

camu.help-template.digest: alternatively, descriptor choice can be sent as list in 2nd inlet
camu.help-template.use_when: Template patcher for authoring new camu.* help files. Skip in production patches — use only as a starting point when adding new help docs to the package.

camu.imubu.control.digest: <-- if we're not using camu.select, we have to reroute some messages directly to mubu.concat (for markers interface)
camu.imubu.control.use_when: Central UI controller — wires together the imubu (Jitter-based corpus visualiser), camu.select selection logic, camu.triggermode, and the various camu.key tagging UIs. Constructor args: `<corpus> <descr>`. 4 inlets (target, descriptor selection, trigger control, etc.), 3 outlets (selection, marker control, display feedback). When you don't use camu.select, target/columns can be sent to middle inlets directly. Use as the central hub of any catart-mubu corpus interface — most other camu.* objects route through it.

camu.key.select.digest: UI module to enable and disable soundfiles for selection and display (works with camu.key.value or camu.key.soundset)
camu.key.select.use_when: UI for enabling/disabling soundfiles by key-value tag during corpus selection. Works in tandem with camu.key.value (manual tagging) or camu.key.soundset (auto-tagging by directory). Constructor arg: `<corpus>`. Use to filter corpus content live — e.g. show only buffers tagged 'instrument:piano' or hide a class of sources.

camu.key.soundset.digest: Tag soundfiles by directory name. UI version incorporated in camu.key.value and both modules work with camu.key.select
camu.key.soundset.use_when: Automatic soundfile tagging by directory name. Buffers loaded from `kick/`, `snare/`, etc. get those folder names as tags automatically. Constructor arg: `<corpus>`. Pairs with camu.key.select to enable/disable tagged groups. Use as the no-effort tagging path when corpus organisation already follows folder structure.

camu.key.value.digest: mubu "target" holds the target vector as the only matrix in track "vector", and uses a pipo plugin to scale the target vector for displaying in the multislider in track "scaled"
camu.key.value.use_when: Manual user-defined key-value tagging UI for soundfiles. Double-click to tag buffers by arbitrary key-value pairs. Constructor arg: `<corpus>`. Pairs with camu.key.select for filtered selection. Holds a target vector as a single-row matrix in mubu track `vector`, with a scaled version in `scaled` for multislider display. Use when corpus organisation can't be derived from filenames or directories.

camu.menu.digest: allows to choose which audio descriptor is affected to which display parameter (x, y, size, colour, etc.) via a menu or number (to go into a bpatcher)
camu.menu.use_when: Descriptor-to-display-parameter assignment menu — choose which audio descriptor drives which visual axis (x, y, size, colour, etc.) of the imubu visualiser. Send a menu choice or numeric index. Available for all display parameters except (x, y) which are the spatial axes themselves. Use when designing a corpus visualisation where descriptor-to-axis mapping should be user-configurable.

camu.random.digest: When either k or radius is >0, knn outputs lists of markers near the target. camu.random then chooses one according to different strategies.
camu.random.use_when: Random selector for the kNN result set — when mubu.knn returns multiple candidates (because k > 1 or radius > 0), camu.random picks one per trigger using a chosen strategy. Constructor takes 4 inlets / 3 outlets configuration. Outputs an `empty` symbol when the selection is empty (since Max has no empty list). Use as the second stage of a corpus selection chain (knn → camu.random → mubu.concat~) for stochastic variation within a target zone.

camu.random.filter.digest: choose among a list of marker/buffer indices according to different random strategies, but only trigger grains when moving cursor and not empty
camu.random.filter.use_when: Filtered random selector — same as camu.random but only triggers when the cursor is moving and the selection is non-empty. Use when you want grains to track cursor motion without re-triggering on stationary cursor (avoids machine-gun grain bursts when not moving). Note: 'Scheduler in Overdrive' must be ON in Audio Status for correct timing. Repetition handling is internal — don't add a separate repetition filter.

camu.record.digest: record signal directly into mubu with live segmentation
camu.record.use_when: Live recording into a MuBu corpus with on-the-fly segmentation. Constructor arg: `<corpus>`. 2 signal inlets (audio, segment trigger), 3 outlets. Records signal directly into mubu and emits markers per segmentation event. Use to add live performance recordings to a corpus during a session — the recording becomes immediately available for kNN selection and granular playback.

camu.select.digest: when not using camu.imubu.control, target and columns can be sent to middle inlets directly
camu.select.use_when: Combined selection logic — handles target descriptor input, column selection, and feedback output for the imubu visualiser in one abstraction. Constructor args: `<corpus> <descr>`. 4 inlets / 2 outlets. Feedback output (2nd outlet) goes into camu.imubu.control's 2nd inlet for visual highlight. When not using camu.imubu.control, target and column messages can go to middle inlets directly. Use as the standard selection wrapper in a catart-mubu voice.

camu.taboo.digest: this is just to keep the period setting in sync between attrui and camu.triggermode
camu.taboo.use_when: Repetition-avoidance filter — suppresses recently-selected marker/buffer indices for a configurable period. Constructor arg: `<period>`. 3 inlets / 3 outlets; the period setting stays in sync with an attached attrui. Use to ensure the corpus selector doesn't keep returning the same grain when the cursor lingers in one zone.

camu.target.digest: alternatively, descriptor choice can be sent as list in 2nd inlet
camu.target.use_when: Target vector + descriptor column manager — formats target descriptor values into the message format mubu.knn expects. Alternative to camu.select when you want explicit control over targeting. 3 inlets / 1 outlet. Descriptor choice indices come from camu.menu's left outlet, or alternately as a list in the 2nd inlet. Use as the lower-level alternative to camu.select when building custom selection chains.

camu.triggermode.digest: select the trigger mode: how mouse movement is translated to playback
camu.triggermode.use_when: Trigger-mode selector — choose how mouse movement translates to grain playback: `cont` (continuous loop), `fence` (trigger on cursor exit from current selection), `click` (only on mouse click). 1 inlet / 2 outlets. Use as the standard trigger control in any catart-mubu voice; pair with camu.cont when in cont mode.

camu.voice.digest: camu.voice can also be used in a bpatcher to show the standard user interface
camu.voice.use_when: Single voice of corpus-based granular playback — wraps mubu.concat~ with the typical catart-mubu signal chain. Constructor args: `<corpus> <descr>`. 2 signal inlets (target, selection), 3 outlets. Can be used as a bpatcher to expose the standard voice UI. Use as the playback engine of a catart-mubu setup; instantiate multiple for polyphony.

cv.jit.binedge.use_when: Find binary edge pixels (ON pixels that have at least one OFF neighbour). Single-plane matrix input, any type — non-zero pixels are treated as ON. Use as the cheapest possible edge extractor when you only need a binary edge mask, not gradient direction or strength. For real edge detection use cv.jit.canny.

cv.jit.blobs.bounds.digest: cv.jit.blobs.bounds offers similar functionality to jit.findbounds but finds the bounding box for every blob in a labeled image.cv.jit.blobs.bounds outputs a 4-plane 1-dimension float32 matrix whose number of cells is the same as the number of blobs in the input image. The planes hold the following information.Plane 1: left coordinatePlane 2: top coordinatePlane 3: right coordinatePlane 4: Bottom coordinate
cv.jit.blobs.bounds.use_when: Bounding box per labelled blob — like jit.findbounds but operates on a labelled-image input from cv.jit.label and returns one bounding box per connected component. `@threshold` controls binary thresholding. Use after cv.jit.label when you need per-blob (left, top, right, bottom) regions for cropping, ROI extraction, or hit-testing.

cv.jit.blobs.centroids.digest: cv.jit.blobs.centroids functions much like cv.jit.centroids except that it takes for input the output of cv.jit.label and calculates the center of mass and area of each connected component individually.The output of cv.jit.label must be of type char.cv.jit.blobs outputs a single-row, 3-plane char matrix where the number of cells is the same as the number of labeled components. The contents of each plane is as follows:Plane 1: x-coordinatePlane 2: y-coordinatePlane 3: area
cv.jit.blobs.centroids.use_when: Centroid (centre of mass) and area per labelled blob — like cv.jit.centroids but per-blob from cv.jit.label output. `@threshold` for binary threshold. Use to track multiple objects' positions and sizes in one frame after labelling.

cv.jit.blobs.direction.digest: cv.jit.blobs.direction is almost identical to cv.jit.blobs.orientation. It also takes in the output of cv.jit.blobs.moments and calculates the orientation of each blob's main axis. However, unlike cv.jit.blobs.orientation, it takes into account symmetry. This means that cv.jit.blobs.direction can tell which direction a connected component is pointing.Like cv.jit.blobs.orientation, the output is in radians by default and can be changed to degrees with the "mode" attribute. The output is between 0 and 2Pi.
cv.jit.blobs.direction.use_when: Per-blob orientation with directional bias — almost identical to cv.jit.blobs.orientation but resolves the 180° ambiguity using mass distribution. Takes cv.jit.blobs.moments output. Use when you need to know which 'end' of an elongated blob points where (e.g. arrow direction, body posture).

cv.jit.blobs.elongation.digest: cv.jit.blobs.elongation computes the elongation of each connected component found by cv.jit.label and analysed by cv.jit.blobs.moments.Elongation is a measure of how thin a shape is. The closer elongation is to 0, the more compact the shape is, like a circle or square. The more line-like the shape becomes, the higher the elongation.cv.jit.blobs.elongation outputs a 1-plane single-row matrix whose number of cells is the same as the input matrix.
cv.jit.blobs.elongation.use_when: Per-blob elongation (thinness measure) from cv.jit.blobs.moments output. Use to filter blobs by shape — distinguish thin objects (high elongation) from compact ones, e.g. fingers vs palms in hand-tracking.

cv.jit.blobs.moments.digest: cv.jit.blobs.moments functions much like cv.jit.moments but computes moments and invariants for every blob identified by cv.jit.label. See cv.jit.moments for a discussion on moments and invariants.The output is a 17-plane, single-row float32 matrix. The number of cells is the same as the number of connected components.Planes 1-7 contain second and third order moments of inertia.Planes 8-14 contain Hu invariant shape descriptorsPlanes 15-16 hold centroid coordinatesPlane 17 holds connected component areaThe output of cv.jit.moments can be fed to other objects for further analysis. See cv.jit.blobs.orientation, cv.jit.blobs.direction, cv.jit.blobs.elongation, and cv.jit.blobs.recon.
cv.jit.blobs.moments.use_when: Per-blob seven-moment invariants — runs cv.jit.moments per labelled blob from cv.jit.label output. Returns one moment list per blob. Use as the analysis stage feeding cv.jit.blobs.elongation / .orientation / .direction / .recon — the standard shape-descriptor pipeline.

cv.jit.blobs.orientation.digest: cv.jit.blobs.orientation functions much like cv.jit.orientation except that it takes for input the output of cv.jit.blobs.moments and calculates the orientation of the main axis of each connected component individually.cv.jit.blobs.orientation outputs a single-row, 1-plane float32 matrix where the number of cells is the same as the number of labeled components. Orientation is measured in radians by default but you can switch to degree output by specifying "mode 1". The values are between 0. and Pi radians, with the extremes being horizontal and Pi/2 vertical.
cv.jit.blobs.orientation.use_when: Per-blob principal-axis orientation (in radians) from cv.jit.blobs.moments output. Resolves to ±π/2; for a directional reading use cv.jit.blobs.direction. Use to track object rotation per frame.

cv.jit.blobs.recon.digest:
cv.jit.blobs.recon calculates the statistical distance between blob shape descriptors and a pre-computed model. The model must be created using cv.jit.learn, and cv.jit.blobs.recon functions much like cv.jit.learn's "compare" mode.
cv.jit.blobs.recon must be fed the output of cv.jit.blobs.moments. Use the "mode" attribute to set whether moments (0) or Hu invariants (1) are used. Make sure that this matches the data used to train the model.
The output is a 1-plane float32 matrix. The lower the output value, the more similar the blob's shape is to the model.
cv.jit.blobs.recon.use_when: Statistical pattern recognition per blob — calculates Mahalanobis-style distance between blob shape descriptors and a pre-trained model. Train the model with cv.jit.learn. Use for shape classification: 'is this blob more like a hand, a face, or noise?' — feed cv.jit.blobs.moments output through a learned model.

cv.jit.blobs.sort.use_when: Re-order blob labels for temporal continuity — same blob keeps the same label across frames where possible. `@threshold` for binary threshold. Use after cv.jit.label to stabilise labels (without sort, blob N in frame 1 may become blob M in frame 2, breaking per-blob tracking).

cv.jit.blur.digest: Sigma specifies the standard deviation of the gaussian kernel. If set to 0, it is calculated automatically based on the radius.
cv.jit.blur.use_when: Gaussian blur. `sigma` attribute sets standard deviation (`0` = auto-calculated from radius); `radius` sets kernel radius. Use as the standard pre-blur step before edge detection or feature analysis to suppress pixel-level noise.

cv.jit.calibration.digest: Number of views to be taken into account for the calibration. At least two but usually several tens for more precision.
cv.jit.calibration.use_when: Camera calibration via multiple chessboard views. `views` attribute caps how many views are aggregated (typically tens for precision). Use to find a camera's intrinsic matrix and distortion coefficients, then feed those into cv.jit.findchessboardcorners → cv.jit.findhomography pipelines for perspective correction.

cv.jit.canny.digest: cv.jit.canny implements the Canny algorithm for edge detections. Sobel, Prewitt and Cross algorithms are already supported in the standard Jitter distribution, however, the Canny method differs from those in that it returns uniquely 1-pixel binary edges. The result is less visually appealing but yields useful information for further analysis. See cv.jit.hough for an example.
cv.jit.canny.use_when: Canny edge detection (the standard edge detector). `threshold` and `range` attrs tune sensitivity — larger threshold = fewer edges. Use for clean edge maps suitable for line detection (Hough), contour finding, or stylised rendering. Better than Sobel/Prewitt (already in standard Jitter) for clean output.

cv.jit.cartopol.use_when: Cartesian → polar conversion for matrix data. 2 inlets / 2 outlets. Pairs with cv.jit.poltocar (inverse). Use to remap (x, y) image data to (r, θ) — useful for radial-symmetric processing or polar-coordinate effects.

cv.jit.centroids.digest: Returns the centroids, or the center of mass of a greyscale image or object.Also returns the mass.
cv.jit.centroids.use_when: Centroid (centre of mass) and total mass of a greyscale or binary image. 1 inlet / 3 outlets (centroid x, y, mass). Use for single-blob tracking (one object in frame); for multi-object use cv.jit.label + cv.jit.blobs.centroids.

cv.jit.changetype.digest: This abstraction changes the type of a matrix, leaving plane count and dimensions unchanged.
cv.jit.changetype.use_when: Change matrix data type (char → float32, etc.) preserving plane count and dimensions. Use as a type-conversion utility before passing to objects with type requirements (e.g. float32 input only).

cv.jit.circularity.digest: cv.jit.circularity computes how compact a shape is. That is to say, circularity is defined as a ration between a shape's area and its perimeter. Theoretically, the most compact shape is the circle. In digital images, however, due to aliasing, shapes such as squares may seem more compact.The higher the circularity, the more compact a shape is, with a perfect circle returning 1. The closer to 0 circularity is, the more jagged is the shape's outline.cv.jit.circularity also returns the perimeter.
cv.jit.circularity.use_when: Compactness measure — ratio of area to perimeter² (theoretical max = 1 for a perfect circle). Use to distinguish round shapes (circles, blobs) from irregular ones (lines, branched shapes) as a single-number descriptor.

cv.jit.close.use_when: Morphological close (dilate then erode). Removes OFF-pixel noise (small holes inside ON regions). Use as a binary-image cleanup step before blob analysis — closes small gaps in object boundaries.

cv.jit.covariance.digest: Calculates the covariance matrix of a vector.For a vector with n elements, returns an n by n matrix.
cv.jit.covariance.use_when: Covariance matrix of a vector. n-element input → n×n covariance matrix output. Use for statistical pattern analysis or feeding shape descriptors into ML pipelines.

cv.jit.dilate.digest: Switch between binary and greyscale mode using grayscale attribute. Works also with "greyscale" spelling!
cv.jit.dilate.use_when: Morphological dilation. `@grayscale 0/1` (also accepts 'greyscale' spelling) switches between binary and greyscale modes. Use to grow ON regions / fill small gaps / make foreground objects larger.

cv.jit.direction.digest: cv.jit.direction: calculate main axis of image
cv.jit.direction.use_when: Calculate the main-axis direction of an image (or blob). `flip` attribute toggles whether the axis points toward the lighter side (like a pointing hand) or the heavier side (like a tail). Use for whole-image directional analysis; for per-blob use cv.jit.blobs.direction.

cv.jit.elongation.digest: cv.jit.elongation receives moments of inertia calculated by cv.jit.moments and computes the elongation of the shape. Elongation quantifies how thin a shape is. High values correspond to thin shapes, like lines, where pixels are grouped close to the main axis (as cv.jit.orientation calculates). On the other hand, the closer elongation is to 0, the more the shape is spread out, like a circle.
cv.jit.elongation.use_when: Whole-image elongation from cv.jit.moments output. Quantifies how thin a shape is. Use to characterise overall image shape with one number; for per-blob use cv.jit.blobs.elongation.

cv.jit.erode.digest: Switch between binary and greyscale mode using grayscale attribute. Works also with "greyscale" spelling!
cv.jit.erode.use_when: Morphological erosion. `@grayscale 0/1` (also accepts 'greyscale') switches modes. Use to shrink ON regions / remove small noise pixels / detach touching objects.

cv.jit.extrema.digest: cv.jit.extrema object is utilized by the cv.jit.hough2lines abstraction
cv.jit.extrema.use_when: Find local extrema in a matrix. Used internally by cv.jit.hough2lines. Use for general peak detection in 2D data — feature points, blob centres, line votes in Hough space.

cv.jit.face.landmarks.digest: The output matrix has 1 row for each face in the image. Each row has 68 columns, with each cell holding the coordinates of the 68 facial landmarks.
cv.jit.face.landmarks.use_when: 68-point facial landmark detector. Output matrix has 1 row per face × 68 columns per landmark (x, y coords). `@normalize` (default 1) scales coords to [0, 1]. Use after cv.jit.faces (or independently if you have face crops) to get detailed face geometry; feed to cv.jit.face.parts for per-feature subsets.

cv.jit.face.parts.digest: This should be connected to the output of cv.jit.face.landmarks.
cv.jit.face.parts.use_when: Slices cv.jit.face.landmarks output into 9 per-feature outlets (eyes, brows, nose, mouth, jawline, etc.). Use to extract just the parts you need — e.g. only lips for lip-sync, only eyes for gaze tracking.

cv.jit.face.rigidpoints.digest: This abstraction filters the data of cv.jit.face.landmarks to only return a few useful "rigid" points. That is, points that should not move regardless of the facial expression.
cv.jit.face.rigidpoints.use_when: Filter cv.jit.face.landmarks down to the 'rigid' points that don't move with expression (typically nose bridge, eye corners, jaw points). Output format matches cv.jit.keypoints. Use for stable face-tracking when you don't want expression to perturb the tracked points.

cv.jit.faces.digest:
cv.jit.faces scans through a greyscale image and tries to find regions that resemble frontal views of human faces. Because the algorithm is rather computationally intensive, it works best on small matrices. Ideally, the smallest face to be detected should be about 20 pixels wide.

While it can be used alone for face tracking, a more efficient use of resources would be to use cv.jit.faces in tandem with a proper tracking object, such as cv.jit.track, only performing face detection when necessary.
cv.jit.faces.use_when: Face detection — scans a greyscale image for frontal-face regions. Outputs (left, top, right, bottom) bounds per face on outlet 0; query face count via `getnfaces` (NOT bare `nfaces` — info-outlet pattern). `model` attr selects detection model (4 built-in models); `read <file>` loads a custom model. Greyscale input only (use jit.rgb2luma upstream). Use for the standard face-detect pipeline; pair with cv.jit.face.landmarks for detailed geometry.

cv.jit.features.digest: cv.jit.features: find features in an image
cv.jit.features.use_when: Find feature points in an image (corners / interest points). `precision 1` enables sub-pixel accuracy at higher CPU cost. Use as a feature detector for tracking or matching; pair with cv.jit.track for optical-flow tracking from these features.

cv.jit.features2track.use_when: Find sticky / trackable features for cv.jit.track — selects high-contrast areas (corners, edges) that survive optical-flow tracking better than smooth regions. 2 inlets (image, params) / 1 outlet. Use as the prep stage before cv.jit.track; produces a feature-points matrix that tracks more reliably than uniform sampling.

cv.jit.findchessboardcorners.digest: The output image blinks when chessboard corners coordinates are taken into account.
cv.jit.findchessboardcorners.use_when: Detect chessboard pattern corners for camera calibration. Output blinks when corners are accepted. 1 inlet / 3 outlets. Pair with cv.jit.calibration to build a calibration. Use as the standard pattern-based camera-calibration step.

cv.jit.findcontours.use_when: Trace contours from a binary raster image — outputs ordered point sequences per closed contour. Use to convert a binary mask into vector-style outlines for shape analysis, polygon approximation, or visual rendering.

cv.jit.findhomography.digest: This external is like cv.jit.getperspective except it needs 1-plane n x 2 matrix input instead of 2-plabe n x 1 due to an OpenCV issue
cv.jit.findhomography.use_when: Find a 3×3 homography matrix from corresponding point pairs — like cv.jit.getperspective but accepts 1-plane n×2 matrix input (vs 2-plane n×1) due to OpenCV API constraints. Use for perspective alignment / image rectification when you have ≥4 corresponding feature points.

cv.jit.floodfill.use_when: Connected-components flood fill. `@seed` attr sets the seed point. Use to label one connected region from a seed — useful for region-grow segmentation or region-of-interest extraction from a click point.

cv.jit.framesub.digest: cv.jit.framesub returns the absolute difference between consecutive frames.
cv.jit.framesub.use_when: Absolute difference between consecutive frames — frame subtraction. Use as the simplest motion detector; threshold the output to get a binary motion mask.

cv.jit.getperspective.digest: This external calculates a matrix of perspective transforms form 4 couples of corresponding points. Useful for tracking calibration.
cv.jit.getperspective.use_when: Compute a perspective-transform matrix from 4 pairs of corresponding points (input is a 2-plane n×1 matrix). Use for perspective correction (de-skewing, projector mapping); pair with jit.perspective or cv.jit.perspective to apply the transform.

cv.jit.hough.digest:
cv.jit.hough computes the Hough space from a binary image. For every ON pixel in the input, every line passing through that point is represented in the output by its distance and angle from the origin. The result is that for every point a sinusoid is created.
Places where many sinusoids intersect correspond to likely lines in the image. See cv.jit.extrema to extract this data.
The resolution of the Hough space representation is regulated bby the two parameter "rho" and "theta" which affect the accuracy of the distance and angle measurement respectively. The actual output matrix dimensions are computed as follows:width = ((input width + input height)*2 + 1) / rhoheight = PI / theta
cv.jit.hough.use_when: Compute the Hough space from a binary edge image. For every ON pixel, every line through that point votes in the Hough space. Use as the analysis stage of line detection; pair with cv.jit.hough2lines (which uses cv.jit.extrema on the Hough output) to extract actual line parameters.

cv.jit.hough2lines.digest: cv.jit.hough2Lines: find lines in Hough space
cv.jit.hough2lines.use_when: Find lines in a Hough space (output of cv.jit.hough). Internally uses cv.jit.extrema. Use as the second stage of explicit Hough-based line detection. For one-shot line detection prefer cv.jit.lines.

cv.jit.hsflow.digest: cv.jit.HSflow computes the optical flow of an incoming greyscale matrix using the Horn-Schunk technique.
cv.jit.hsflow.use_when: Optical flow via Horn-Schunck algorithm. Greyscale matrix input. Output is a 2-plane float32 flow field (horizontal, vertical). Use for dense optical flow when you need flow at every pixel; for sparse use cv.jit.lkflow or cv.jit.track.

cv.jit.iter.digest: This works a lot like jit.iter but instead outputs one row or one column of the input matrix at a time.
cv.jit.iter.use_when: Output one row or one column of an input matrix at a time — like jit.iter but row/column granularity instead of cell. The index is the row/column to extract. Use to step through matrix data row-wise without writing a per-cell iterator.

cv.jit.kalman.use_when: Kalman filter optimised for 2D tracking (factors in velocity and acceleration). 1 inlet / 1 outlet. Use to smooth and predict tracked-point positions — much more stable than raw cv.jit.track output, especially through brief occlusions.

cv.jit.keypoints.digest: Adjust the threshold to change the number of features detected. Be careful, very small threshold values will return a verly large number of features. Usually between 0.05 and 0.3.
cv.jit.keypoints.use_when: Detect keypoints (interest points) for image matching. `threshold` adjusts feature density (smaller = more features); `@normalize 1` rescales coords to [0, 1] for cross-resolution matching. 1 inlet / 3 outlets. Use as the modern feature detector for image matching pipelines (vs older cv.jit.features); pair with cv.jit.keypoints.match.

cv.jit.keypoints.match.digest: Use cv.jit.unproject to find the position relative to the camera.
cv.jit.keypoints.match.use_when: Match keypoints between two images for finding correspondences. 4 inlets (two image keypoint sets + their descriptors) / 3 outlets. Use cv.jit.unproject to convert matches to camera-relative positions. Use for image-stitching, object recognition, structure-from-motion-style applications.

cv.jit.label.use_when: Connected-components labelling — assigns a unique label to each connected ON region in a binary image. `@charmode 0/1` and `@mode` control labelling behaviour. Use as the prerequisite for any cv.jit.blobs.* analysis (those operate on labelled images).

cv.jit.learn.digest:
cv.jit.learn is a tool for pattern recognition similar, though more elaborated, to cv.jit.undergrad. The user trains the object by feeding lists in its right inlet. These lists are assumed to desribe the state of something to be recognized. Normally, this will be moments of inertia or Hu invariants, as calculated by cv.jit.moments, but could be anything. If the size of the list is not 7, you need to specify it as an argument to the object.
Care should be taken during the training phase, as the quality of the training data will affect the accuracy of the recognition stage.
Once a model has been trained it can be saved using the "write" message. Saved models can be loaded with the "read" message.
When lists are sent to the left inlet, the statistical distance between the current state and the model is computed and output. By setting an appropriate threshold, the user can determine if the candidate list matches the training data.
Models created by cv.jit.learn can also be used by cv.jit.blobs.recon.
cv.jit.learn.use_when: Pattern-recognition trainer (more capable than cv.jit.undergrad). Train by feeding lists into the right inlet (training mode); switch to comparison mode to classify new lists. The training/comparison toggle is configurable via attribute. Use to build classifiers from feature lists (e.g. cv.jit.moments output) — pair with cv.jit.blobs.recon for shape recognition.

cv.jit.lines.digest: cv.jit.lines uses the Hough space to find straight lines in a greyscale image. First, the Canny algorithm is used to find image edges. The edge image is then transformed into a Hough representation, from which line candidate features are extracted. The output of cv.jit.lines is a 4-plane matrix of long type. The number of cells in the matrix corresponds to the number of lines found in the image. The four planes describe the position of the line segments in the following manner:Plane 1: Left-most x coordinatePlane 2: Top-most y coordinatePlane 3: Right-most x coordinatePlane 4: Bottom-most y coordinateA utility abstraction, cv.jit.lines.draw, is provided to easily draw the output of cv.jit.lines onto an image.
cv.jit.lines.use_when: One-shot straight-line detection — runs Canny then Hough internally. `gap` attr = max gap in a broken line; `length` attr = min line length. Use for direct line detection on greyscale images without manual Canny/Hough plumbing.

cv.jit.lkflow.digest: cv.jit.LKflow computes the optical flow of an incoming greyscale matrix using the Lucas-Kanade technique.Quality can be controled via the "radius" attribute, although large values will result in a heavier CPU load.
cv.jit.lkflow.use_when: Optical flow via Lucas-Kanade algorithm. `radius` controls quality. Output: 2-plane float32 (plane 0 = horizontal flow, plane 1 = vertical flow). Use for sparse / window-based optical flow; faster than Horn-Schunck for many tracking applications.

cv.jit.lowpass.digest: Use the mouse position as a source. Try moving the mouse very quickly back and forth.
cv.jit.lowpass.use_when: First-order low-pass filter for matrix data over time. `@cutoff` sets cutoff frequency; reset message reinitialises filter state. Use to smooth jittery tracked-point data (mouse, blob centroids) — cheaper than cv.jit.kalman when you don't need velocity prediction.

cv.jit.mass.use_when: Sum of all pixel values in a matrix (total 'mass'). 1 inlet / 2 outlets (mass, pass-through). Use for one-number activity detection or as the denominator in centroid/normalisation calculations.

cv.jit.mean.digest: Calculates the mean matrix value over time.Note that cv.jit.mean now accurately calculates char matrices, so there is no need to convert to float for accuracy.
cv.jit.mean.use_when: Time-domain pixel-wise mean — running average of a matrix stream. Now char-accurate (no float conversion needed). Use to model a static background for background-subtraction-style motion detection.

cv.jit.module.presence.digest: Raise the lo slider to tune out background activity so that you get a clean 0 on the ouput. Lower the hi slider to define a condition of maximum activitiy or presence near the camera.
cv.jit.module.presence.use_when: Max for Live presence-detection module — outputs CC1 in M4L MIDI Effect context. Lo slider tunes background; hi slider sets max-activity reference. Use as a drop-in M4L device for camera-based presence sensing in Live sets.

cv.jit.moments.digest: cv.jit.moments is the basic object for shape analysis. It computes a list of seven moments, or shape descriptors. Refer to the documentation for a more detailed explanation of these descriptors.Those descriptors are said to be "central normalized" moments. That means that for the same shape, the values remain constant regardless of size or position.cv.jit.moments also computes a list of seven invariant shape descriptors. Like moments, they remain constant despite translation and scale. However, unlike moments, they are also invariant to rotation.See cv.jit.orientation, cv.jit.direction, cv.jit.elongation and cv.jit.learn for uses of moments.
cv.jit.moments.use_when: Compute the seven Hu image moments (shape descriptors) for a single image. 1 inlet / 5 outlets. Use as the analysis stage feeding cv.jit.elongation / .orientation / .direction. For per-blob analysis use cv.jit.blobs.moments after cv.jit.label.

cv.jit.noise.digest: Every dimension can have different parameters. Here, the first two dimensions are used as coordinates and the third as the hue.
cv.jit.noise.use_when: Per-dimension noise generator — different distribution parameters per axis. `@mode` selects distribution; specify per-dimension settings or use the same across all. Use to generate textured noise for testing, dithering, or as a procedural texture source.

cv.jit.notempty.digest: This matrix only has one cell. It is empty if this cell's value is 0.
cv.jit.notempty.use_when: Single-cell test — outputs 0 if input is empty (single-cell value 0), 1 otherwise. Use as a gate before downstream processing to skip empty frames or absent tracking data.

cv.jit.open.use_when: Morphological open (erode then dilate). Removes ON-pixel noise (specks). Use as a binary-image cleanup step — opens small gaps between objects and removes tiny isolated bright pixels.

cv.jit.opticalflow.digest: Double-click these patchers to read more about each algorithm and see what kind of parameters you can adjust.
cv.jit.opticalflow.use_when: Unified optical-flow front-end with multiple algorithm choices (Horn-Schunck, Lucas-Kanade, etc. — double-click the help patchers for details). `@unique 1` recommended (sending the same matrix twice yields zero flow). Use as the convenience wrapper instead of choosing between cv.jit.hsflow / cv.jit.lkflow directly.

cv.jit.orientation.digest: cv.jit.orientation calculates the orientation of a shape's main axis. You need to compute moments of inertia beforehand.The orientation is calculated in radians, with 0 and Pi radians corresponding to a horizontal line. Pi/2 radians is vertical. Note that cv.jit.orientation only returns values between 0. and Pi.
cv.jit.orientation.use_when: Whole-image principal-axis orientation in radians (requires upstream cv.jit.moments). Use for whole-image rotation tracking; for per-blob use cv.jit.blobs.orientation.

cv.jit.perimeter.digest: cv.jit.perimeter counts the number of edge pixels in a binary image, that is, the number of ON pixels that have at least one OFF neighbour.
cv.jit.perimeter.use_when: Count edge pixels (ON pixels with at least one OFF neighbour) — perimeter length of a binary shape. Use to compute shape complexity or as the denominator in cv.jit.circularity-style ratios.

cv.jit.perspective.digest: there are 3 points for this example but you can put as many as you like
cv.jit.perspective.use_when: Apply a perspective transform to matrix data. 2 inlets (image, transform matrix) / 2 outlets. Use to warp an image after cv.jit.findhomography or cv.jit.getperspective produces the transform — projector keystone, table-top corner mapping.

cv.jit.poltocar.digest: Thesse abstractions work just like the regular Max objects cartopol and poltocar on matrix data.
cv.jit.poltocar.use_when: Polar → Cartesian conversion for matrix data. Inverse of cv.jit.cartopol. Use to undo polar transforms or generate radial patterns in (r, θ) and convert to (x, y) for display.

cv.jit.ravg.digest: cv.jit.ravg: calculate the running average over time
cv.jit.ravg.use_when: Running average over time (smoothing). `@alpha` sets the smoothing weight. Similar to cv.jit.mean but with explicit alpha control rather than uniform averaging. Use as a cheap temporal smoother for matrix streams.

cv.jit.resize.digest: cv.jit.resize output is anti-aliased compared to jit.matrix.
cv.jit.resize.use_when: Anti-aliased matrix resize — better quality than jit.matrix's interp resize. `@size <w> <h>` sets target dimensions. Use whenever you need quality resampling (e.g. resizing tracked image regions for analysis).

cv.jit.shift.use_when: Track an image area via continuously adaptive mean shift. Click-and-drag on the input pwindow to set the search window's starting position. 1 inlet / 4 outlets (position, size, etc.). Use for object tracking initialised by user selection — e.g. select a face once, track it as it moves.

cv.jit.stddev.digest: cv.jit.stddev computes the standard deviation over time for every pixel of the incoming matrices. The standard deviation is a statistical measure of how spread out from the mean the data is. For a normal distribution, about 68% of samples are within 1 standard deviations of the mean, 95% within 2 and 99.7% within 3 standard deviations.The standard deviation can be useful for filtering and recognition tasks like background subtraction: in the background analysis stage, the standard deviation is computed. In the comparison stage, pixels are compared to the standard deviation, if they are more than, for instance, 2 * the standard deviation, they can be considered foreground.
cv.jit.stddev.use_when: Pixel-wise standard deviation over time. Use to detect unusual variation per pixel — useful for change detection more sensitive than simple frame difference.

cv.jit.sum.use_when: Total pixel sum across a matrix. 1 inlet / 2 outlets. Use for whole-frame activity measurement or as a normalisation denominator.

cv.jit.threshold.use_when: Adaptive thresholding (local, not global). `@mode` selects algorithm, `@radius` sets neighbourhood size, `@threshold` sets bias. Use when global thresholding fails (uneven lighting) — adaptive threshold compares each pixel to its local neighbourhood.

cv.jit.touches.digest: A series of lists containing the same information as the first outlet but for LOST objects. The coordinates are the last valid values.
cv.jit.touches.use_when: Multi-touch / multi-blob tracker with track-IDs. `@radius` sets matching radius. Outputs lists of (id, x, y) for active touches and a separate list for LOST touches with their last valid coords. Use for multi-finger or multi-blob tracking with persistent IDs across frames — built on cv.jit.label internally.

cv.jit.track.use_when: Optical-flow-based point tracking. `@npoints` caps number of tracked points. Right inlet receives the image to draw frame-to-frame displacement on (use `frgb` attr for colour). Use to track features detected by cv.jit.features2track over time — sparse motion tracking.

cv.jit.undergrad.digest: cv.jit.undergrad performs very simple pattern analysis and recognition on an input list. Usually, this list will be the output of cv.jit.moments.The user must first train the object by sending lists that correspond to "positives" in its right inlet. The mean value and standard deviation of each list element is computed. When a list is sent to its left inlet, it is compared with the mean vector computed in the training phase. The object then counts the number of elements that are within 2.5 standard deviations of the mean and outputs the result. For instance, if using the output of cv.jit.moments, input lists have 7 elements. An output of 7 from cv.jit.undergrad would mean that every element is within bounds of the training data, thus almost certainly a positive hit.
cv.jit.undergrad.use_when: Simpler pattern recognition than cv.jit.learn. Use to test classification ideas with minimal setup. The number of list elements within 2.5 standard deviations of the mean is reported as the match. For production use cv.jit.learn.

cv.jit.unproject.digest: Here we paint each point with the value of its index. This simulates the effect of cv.jit.label, while also ensuring that each blob's label doesn't change as the shape is rotated.
cv.jit.unproject.use_when: Convert 2D image coordinates to 3D camera-relative coordinates using calibration data. 2 inlets / 3 outlets. Use after cv.jit.calibration to convert tracked image points into a 3D camera frame — necessary for AR / spatial-projection workflows.

cv.jit.variance.digest: cv.jit.variance computes the standard deviation over time for every pixel of the incoming matrices. The variance is a statistical measure of how spread out from the mean the data is. See cv.jit.stddev for a related, easier to use, measurement.
cv.jit.variance.use_when: Pixel-wise variance over time (square of cv.jit.stddev). Use as the variance form of stddev when downstream math expects variance rather than standard deviation.

dada.analysis.centroid.use_when: Symbolic centroid. Bach-shaped data — see package_concepts.md bach + cage/dada sections for llll/midicent conventions.

dada.analysis.chroma.use_when: Symbolic chroma. Bach-shaped data — see package_concepts.md bach + cage/dada sections for llll/midicent conventions.

dada.analysis.count.use_when: Count items. Bach-shaped data — see package_concepts.md bach + cage/dada sections for llll/midicent conventions.

dada.analysis.duration.use_when: Duration. Bach-shaped data — see package_concepts.md bach + cage/dada sections for llll/midicent conventions.

dada.analysis.energy.use_when: Symbolic energy. Bach-shaped data — see package_concepts.md bach + cage/dada sections for llll/midicent conventions.

dada.analysis.minmax.digest: Minimum and maximum of a parameter
dada.analysis.minmax.use_when: Minimum and maximum of a parameter. Bach-shaped data — see package_concepts.md bach + cage/dada sections for llll/midicent conventions.

dada.analysis.rhythmdistrib.use_when: Rhythmic energy distribution. Bach-shaped data — see package_concepts.md bach + cage/dada sections for llll/midicent conventions.

dada.analysis.spread.use_when: Symbolic spread. Bach-shaped data — see package_concepts.md bach + cage/dada sections for llll/midicent conventions.

dada.base.use_when: SQLite database. Bach-shaped data — see package_concepts.md bach + cage/dada sections for llll/midicent conventions.

dada.bodies.use_when: Music via gravitation. Bach-shaped data — see package_concepts.md bach + cage/dada sections for llll/midicent conventions.

dada.boids.use_when: Swarm intelligence modeling. Bach-shaped data — see package_concepts.md bach + cage/dada sections for llll/midicent conventions.

dada.bounce.use_when: Bounce balls in 2D chambers. Bach-shaped data — see package_concepts.md bach + cage/dada sections for llll/midicent conventions.

dada.cartesian.use_when: Cartesian display of database. Bach-shaped data — see package_concepts.md bach + cage/dada sections for llll/midicent conventions.

dada.catart.use_when: Symbolic concatenative synthesis interface. Bach-shaped data — see package_concepts.md bach + cage/dada sections for llll/midicent conventions.

dada.distances.use_when: Distance-based representation of points. Bach-shaped data — see package_concepts.md bach + cage/dada sections for llll/midicent conventions.

dada.graph.use_when: Graph network. Bach-shaped data — see package_concepts.md bach + cage/dada sections for llll/midicent conventions.

ej.4m.digest: output the min, median, mean, maximum on a int/float stream
ej.4m.use_when: Output min/median/mean/max statistics on an int/float stream. Optional 2nd arg `<verbose>` (default 0) for diagnostic prints. Use as a one-shot statistical summary on a control-rate value stream — single-number version of ej.mmmm (which works on lists). Native equivalent: peak~/avg~ + custom median + minmax — substantial.

ej.buffer2l.digest: look at this patch in presentation in Mira
ej.buffer2l.use_when: Extract 'meaningful' samples out of a buffer~ as a list — content-aware sampling rather than uniform decimation. Designed for inspection in a Mira-mirrored presentation view. Use to pull a representative subset of a buffer's samples into list-domain processing for visualisation, feature extraction, or debugging.

ej.cc.digest: arg (optional): recursive mode (0 disable, 1 enable - default)
ej.cc.use_when: Cross-clear utility (JS). Optional arg: recursive mode (0 disable, 1 enable, default 1). Use for clearing connected/related objects in a patcher graph as a single operation — bulk clear without enumerating targets manually.

ej.cn.digest: change name of a named (via the Object>Name... command) object
ej.cn.use_when: Change the name of a named object (one set via Object > Name…). Use to programmatically rename receive/send/value/coll/etc. targets at runtime — e.g. swap which named object a `value` reads from without re-instantiating.

ej.cycle.digest: cycle send only the message to the outlet, the arguments are dispatched over the others outlets.
ej.cycle.use_when: Send a message's selector to outlet 0 and dispatch its arguments across the remaining outlets. Inverse of pack — splits a structured message into selector + per-arg streams. Use as a low-cost selector/argument splitter without writing a route + unpack chain.

ej.dialog.digest: Open a dialog box for text entry
ej.dialog.use_when: Open a system dialog box for text entry. Constructor args: `<prefix> <default-text>`. 1 inlet (trigger), 2 outlets (text, status). Use when you need user-typed text input mid-patch (filenames, OSC addresses, presets) without building a custom UI.

ej.dn.digest: argument (optional): 1 search a named object which contains the string
ej.dn.use_when: Display named objects (those with Object > Name…) — lists what's named in the patch. Optional arg: `1` to filter by name substring. Note: place `ej.dn.js` at the top level of the main patch for full visibility. Use for debugging — find all the named hooks in a complex patch.

ej.dx2x.use_when: Create a list from a list of intervals — running-sum, inverse of ej.x2dx. Send a list of deltas, get back the cumulative values. Use for converting a relative pattern (interval list) into absolute positions (cumulative list) — useful for rhythmic / pitch transformations.

ej.fplay.digest: ej.fplay uses the same messages as the standart function object:
ej.fplay.use_when: Non-graphical multi-breakpoint function player — same message API as native `function` but no UI. Send `set <list>` to load points, drive a phasor in to play through, dump second function via the dump outlet. Use when function's UI footprint isn't wanted (encapsulated subpatchers, presentation-mode hidden logic) but the message API is.

ej.ftom.digest: args:- float tuning (default: 440 Hz)- int MIDI pitch reference (default: 69)
ej.ftom.use_when: Frequency (Hz) → MIDI note number. Args: `<tuning-Hz>` (default 440), `<MIDI-reference>` (default 69). Use as the inverse of mtof; respects custom tuning standards. Native: ftom~ exists for signal-rate; ej.ftom is control-rate with explicit tuning args.

ej.function.digest: ej.function uses the same messages as the standart function object:
ej.function.use_when: Graphical multi-breakpoint function editor (JS) — same message API as native function plus 'move modes' (Martin Robinson). Drag-mode behaviour can be configured beyond the built-in option. Use when native function's drag behaviour is too limiting (constrained-axis dragging, snap modes, etc.).

ej.jit.preview.digest: Jitter Patcher Window (with slowing down feature)
ej.jit.preview.use_when: Jitter Patcher Window preview (bpatcher) with frame-rate slowing for inspection. Create via the bpatcher's prototypes menu. Use to embed a low-frame-rate preview of a Jitter pipeline inside a larger patch without spawning a full jit.window.

ej.l2buffer.use_when: Copy a list into a buffer~. `set <buffer-name> <channel>` selects destination, then send the list to write. Use as a list→buffer bridge — load a feature curve or pattern into a buffer for signal-rate consumption (similar to fluid.list2buf but lighter, no FluCoMa dep).

ej.lchange.digest: You can define the mode attribute with an integer or a symbol:- "mode 0" is equivalent to "mode thru" (default)- "mode 1" is equivalent to "mode bang"- "mode 2" is equivalent to "mode int".
ej.lchange.use_when: List-aware `change` — outputs only when the input list differs from the previous. `mode` attr selects what to output: `0`/`thru` (pass changed lists through, default), `1`/`bang` (bang only), `2` (custom). Works efficiently on big lists. Use for change detection on list streams where native change can't help.

ej.lclip.digest: Constrains a list within a certain range
ej.lclip.use_when: Clamp every element of a list to a min/max range. Args: `<min> <max>`. Use as the list-domain equivalent of native `clip` — apply per-element clamping without zl + iter chains.

ej.ldrunk.digest: args:- min (optional, 0 if not present)- max- random step: ± (step / 2)- % of random's probability
ej.ldrunk.use_when: List-domain drunk — random walk on every element of a list. Args: `<min> <max> <step> <probability>`. `interval` attr enables continuous output; `ignore` and `autoreset` attrs control zero-value handling. Use for evolving multi-channel parameter streams (e.g. driving 16 controls with correlated random walks) instead of N parallel drunk objects.

ej.led.digest: Fun "meter" effects can be achieved by clever setting of the led "flash time" in the Get Info… dialog.
ej.led.use_when: LED indicator UI (JS). 'Flash time' attribute (Get Info dialog) controls fade behaviour for meter-style effects. `pict <N>` changes colour immediately. Use as a denser, scriptable alternative to native LED display — better for VU-meter-style multi-state indicators.

ej.line.digest: Note:there is some timing problem (it's getting shorter while the number of segment is getting higher). You can attenuate that problem while using a smaller grain size.
ej.line.use_when: Multi-segment line~ generator with timing-grain control. Constructor args: `<initial-value> <segment-count>`. Right inlet sets time grain (output interval, default 20 ms); shorter grain reduces timing drift on long segment counts. 3 inlets / 2 outlets. Use when native line/line~ isn't precise enough for your use case and you can afford a smaller output interval.

ej.linterp.digest: with autotrigger on, each input trigger the interpolation
ej.linterp.use_when: List interpolator with per-trigger autotrigger mode. Hold shift while dragging to change interpolation distance. Use for crossfading between list-domain values (preset blends, parameter morphs). Compare to native `slide` (signal-rate, single-value) — ej.linterp is list-rate with explicit trigger control.

ej.lock.digest: Note that polling doesn't always work since Max 5 because, the patcher might have multiple views with different lock states.
ej.lock.use_when: Patcher lock-state monitor (JS). Optional arg: polling interval (default 0). Note: polling has limitations since Max 5 because patchers can have multiple views with different lock states. Use to expose the patcher's locked/unlocked state to in-patch logic — e.g. show different UI behaviour depending on edit mode.

ej.lop.digest: sin: sinecos: cosinetan: tangentasin: arcsineacos: arccosineatan: arctangentatan2: arctangent(binary)
ej.lop.use_when: List-domain math/trig operator. Op messages: `sin`, `cos`, `tan`, `asin`, `acos`, `atan`, `atan2`, plus arithmetic. Op can be set via attribute too. Use as an example of list-domain math — but for production use prefer ej.linterp or zl-based chains for efficiency.

ej.lquant.digest: Quantize a stream of number or a list (sort of multiple rounding process)
ej.lquant.use_when: Quantize a list (or stream of numbers) to a step size — multi-rounding. Use to snap multi-element parameter lists to a grid simultaneously (e.g. quantize 16 control values to a scale).

ej.lscale.digest: arg1 is input low value (default 0.)arg2 is input high value(default 127.)arg3 is output low value (default 0.)arg4 is output high value (default 1.)arg5 is exponent value (default 1. = linear)
ej.lscale.use_when: List-domain `scale` — map an input range to an output range per-element with optional clipping. Args: `<in-low> <in-high> <out-low> <out-high> <exponent>`. `clip` attr constrains output to the target range. Use as the list-rate version of native scale — apply a single mapping to a parameter list in one object.

ej.lslide.digest: When interval attribute is set to a value greater than 0, it outputs lists continuously.
ej.lslide.use_when: List-domain `slide` — applies the slide algorithm `y(n) = y(n-1) + ((x(n) - y(n-1))/slide)` per-list-element. `interval` attr enables continuous output. Use for smoothing a multi-channel parameter stream where each channel needs the same slide behaviour.

ej.lui.digest: ej.lui is a self-destructive object. It kills itself after creating a jsui objet. This allows you to quickly create jsui objects with custom javascript file.
ej.lui.use_when: Self-destructive jsui factory — `js ej.lui.js <jsui-filename>` in an object box auto-creates a jsui with the given JS file and replaces itself. Use as a quick way to author custom jsui objects without typing out the full jsui invocation each time.

ej.mmmm.digest: output the min, median, mean, maximum on a int/float stream
ej.mmmm.use_when: List-domain min/median/mean/max statistics (newer C-based version of ej.4m, faster via zl modes). Use as a one-stop list-summary object — outputs four statistics from one input list.

ej.mout~.digest: offset 0 turns off the automatic offset (where the spatialization is centered between the first and second speaker)
ej.mout~.use_when: Multichannel signal output / spatialiser. Args: `<num-outputs>` (2–16), `<offset>`. `@offset 0` disables the automatic centring offset (where spatialisation centres between the first two speakers). Use for ad-hoc multichannel spatialisation routing without setting up mc.* infrastructure.

ej.mtof.digest: args:- float tuning (default: 440 Hz)- int MIDI pitch reference (default: 69)
ej.mtof.use_when: MIDI note → frequency (Hz), with custom tuning. Args: `<tuning-Hz>` (default 440), `<MIDI-reference>` (default 69). Use when native `mtof` doesn't accept tuning arguments — e.g. A=432 Hz tuning systems, microtonal references.

ej.nthru.digest: it does nothing... just pass the input to the output
ej.nthru.use_when: No-op passthrough — input passes unchanged to outlet. Use as a debugging stub or as a placeholder when patching a chain you'll fill in later. Trivial but useful for keeping wiring stable during prototyping.

ej.numbox.digest: you can use the tabulation key to pass to the next number box...
ej.numbox.use_when: Number box with tab-key navigation between instances. Tab moves keyboard focus to next; `select` message focuses one programmatically. Use when native `number` / `flonum` lack the tab-navigation needed for fast multi-field data entry (forms, parameter dialogs).

ej.op.digest: argument (optional): 1 search a named object which contains the string
ej.op.use_when: Object-search utility (JS). Optional args: `1` to find named objects whose name contains a substring; `1` (2nd arg) for case-insensitive search. Use to programmatically locate named objects by partial name — useful in dynamically-built patches.

ej.route.use_when: Alternative `route` implementation (JS, e--j). Use as a drop-in alternative when native route's behaviour doesn't fit (e.g. need different fall-through semantics).

ej.sliders-overview.digest: 3 sizes (big, small, tiny)2 colors (Aqua, Graphite)2 backgrounds (Aqua, Metal)2 orientations (vertical, horizontal)
ej.sliders-overview.use_when: Reference patch — sliders in 3 sizes (big/small/tiny), 2 colours (Aqua/Graphite), 2 backgrounds, 2 orientations. Skip in production — open in editor as a copy-paste reference for the available slider styles.

ej.urn.digest: takes two optional arguments: first sets maximum (default 1), second sets random number seed (default 0)
ej.urn.use_when: Random-without-repetition (urn-style draw). Args: `<max>` (default 1), `<seed>` (default 0). `autoclear` attr re-fills the urn when exhausted. Right outlet bangs when all numbers have been drawn. Use as native urn but with autoclear and seed control built in.

ej.vdb-sig~.digest: Variable delay without clicks or transposition
ej.vdb-sig~.use_when: Signal-rate variable delay without clicks or transposition. 3 signal inlets (input, delay-time, window-size), 1 outlet. Use for time-modulated delay where artefacts from delay-time changes (clicks, pitch shifts) must be suppressed — useful for chorus, flanger, or pitch-correct delay sweeps. Signal-rate version of ej.vdb~.

ej.vdb~.digest: Variable delay without clicks or transposition
ej.vdb~.use_when: Variable delay without clicks or transposition. Args: `<window-size> <delay-time>`. 3 inlets / 1 outlet. Use as a click-free, pitch-stable variable delay — drop-in for tapout~ when you need to sweep delay time without artefacts.

ej.x2dx.digest: get the interval between successive values of a list
ej.x2dx.use_when: Get the interval between successive values of a list (running differences) — inverse of ej.dx2x. Use to convert absolute values to relative deltas (e.g. note onset times → inter-onset intervals).

jit.fullscreen.digest: If there is a second monitor, the window will be displayed on it. Press Escape key to toggle the fullscreen mode.
jit.fullscreen.use_when: Fullscreen window abstraction. Args: `<x-dim> <y-dim> <jit.window-attrs>` (e.g. `640 480 1 700 40`). `@interp` and `@pos` attrs available. Press Escape to toggle fullscreen. Multi-monitor: opens on the second monitor if present. Use as the standard fullscreen output for Jitter patches without configuring jit.window manually.

jit.getparam.digest: arg: name of the receive connected to the jitter object
jit.getparam.use_when: Get parameters from a Jitter object via its receive name. Constructor arg: `<receive-name>` matching the target jitter object's `name` attr. 2 inlets / 3 outlets. `defaults` message queries object defaults (works on most but not all Jitter objects). Use to programmatically inspect a Jitter object's current parameter values.

kaf.use_when: macOS-only utility — keep audio focused / app-active. 1 inlet / 0 outlets. Use only on macOS. Skip on Windows/Linux.

kd&u.digest: Output is 1 when the desired key is down, 0 when it's going up…
kd&u.use_when: Key-down-and-up event detector. Args: `<keycode>` (default 32 = space), `<modifiers>` (default 0 mac, 128 windows). 0 inlets / 3 outlets. Outputs `1` on key down, `0` on key up. Use for keyboard-driven control surfaces; lower-level than native key (event-based with explicit up/down tracking).

ptt.digest: first: maximum slider value second : minimum value when you're talking
ptt.use_when: Push-to-talk volume control abstraction. Args: `<max-slider-value>` (default 127), `<min-while-talking>` (default 90). Press ctrl+cmd to change max. Use for video-conferencing setups where mic level should auto-duck when not pressing.

splay~.use_when: Soundfile player abstraction. Arg: `<num-channels>` (default 2). Keyboard shortcuts: shift+/ start/stop, shift+- pause toggle (numeric pad keys). Use as a drop-in stereo/multi-channel file player with built-in transport keys; lighter than playlist~.

stout~.digest: Since Max 5, in order to use the shortcuts, you need to press the ctrl key, then the shortcut letter.
stout~.use_when: Stereo sound output controller. Args: `<num-channels>` (default 2), `<num-banks>` (default 6). Note: in Max 5+ keyboard shortcuts require holding ctrl first. Use as a fully-featured stereo output stage (mute, gain, AB switch) when ezdac~ isn't enough but you don't want to roll a custom output bus.

filter.butterworth.digest: Nth-order Butterworth filterThis object is intended mainly for plotting
filter.butterworth.use_when: Nth-order Butterworth filterThis object is intended mainly for plotting.

filter.chebyshev1.digest: Nth-order Chebyshev Type-I filterThis object is intended mainly for plotting
filter.chebyshev1.use_when: Nth-order Chebyshev Type-I filterThis object is intended mainly for plotting.

filter.chebyshev2.use_when: Nth-order Chebyshev Type-II filterThis object is intended mainly for plotting.

filter.elliptic.use_when: Nth-order elliptic filterThis object is intended mainly for plotting.

filter.legendre.use_when: Nth-order Legendre filterThis object is intended mainly for plotting.

grainflow.scrubSynth~.use_when: Create a noise synthesizer through granular synthesis.  The scrubSynth uses integer multiples of frequency and traversal to generate a wavelet synthesis-like approach using granulation.

grainflow.spat.format.use_when: Formats grain position messages for other systems such as Spat Gris or the IEM Suite.

strncmp.use_when: compare two strings for n chars - arguments: string 2 (symbol), num chars (int, < 4096), mode (0/1) default 0. jasch utility object — geometric/algorithmic helper.

strncpy.use_when: string n character copy - arguments: string 2 (symbol) default <none>, mode (0/1) default 0. jasch utility object — geometric/algorithmic helper.

strtok.use_when: slice string into tokens at delimiter - arguments: string 2 (symbol), mode (0/1). jasch utility object — geometric/algorithmic helper.

sumlist.use_when: calculate the sum of a list. jasch utility object — geometric/algorithmic helper.

timestamp.use_when: get sysdate & time and ms. jasch utility object — geometric/algorithmic helper.

timex.use_when: convert time to ms and vice-versa, outputs as float, list or symbol - argument (int, optional): display mode, default 0: set <symbol>. jasch utility object — geometric/algorithmic helper.

toascii.use_when: convert atom(s) to ascii code. jasch utility object — geometric/algorithmic helper.

toxine.use_when: read and write file from/to disk by lines. jasch utility object — geometric/algorithmic helper.

xframe~.digest: xframe~ generates a list of the amplitude in all the FFT-bins below Nyquist & statistics on spectrum - arguments: <int> FFT-size
xframe~.use_when: xframe~ generates a list of the amplitude in all the FFT-bins below Nyquist & statistics on spectrum - arguments: <int> FFT-size. jasch utility object — geometric/algorithmic helper.
